title: MBLEx D3 Pathology, Contraindications & Special Populations v3 (prompt-locked, aligned distractors)

questions.prompt: A client reports sudden chest pressure with shortness of breath and nausea. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Ask the client to rest for 10 minutes and continue if symptoms improve.
choices.B: Proceed with light massage to reduce stress.
choices.C: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
choices.D: Recommend an over-the-counter anti-inflammatory and continue.

questions.answer: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: Possible cardiac symptoms are a medical emergency; end care and seek immediate emergency evaluation. This prioritizes safety and appropriate referral/modification.
questions.trap: Waiting to see if it improves delays emergency care and increases risk.

questions.prompt: A client reports sudden chest pressure with shortness of breath and nausea. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Proceed with light massage to reduce stress.
choices.B: Ask the client to rest for 10 minutes and continue if symptoms improve.
choices.C: Perform deep work on the upper back to 'open the chest'.
choices.D: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).

questions.answer: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: Possible cardiac symptoms are a medical emergency; end care and seek immediate emergency evaluation.
questions.trap: Waiting to see if it improves delays emergency care and increases risk.

questions.prompt: During intake, a client reports new unilateral calf swelling, warmth, and tenderness after a long flight. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Recommend stretching and continue the session.
choices.B: Do not treat; refer for urgent medical evaluation due to possible deep vein thrombosis.
choices.C: Use deep stripping to break up the tightness in the calf.
choices.D: Perform vigorous massage to improve circulation in the leg.

questions.answer: Do not treat; refer for urgent medical evaluation due to possible deep vein thrombosis.
questions.why: Unilateral swelling/warmth after prolonged immobility is a red flag for DVT; massage is contraindicated and referral is indicated.
questions.trap: Vigorous massage can dislodge a clot and is unsafe.

questions.prompt: During intake, a client reports new unilateral calf swelling, warmth, and tenderness after a long flight. What is the What should you do NEXT?

choices.A: Do not treat; refer for urgent medical evaluation due to possible deep vein thrombosis.

questions.trap: Vigorous massage can dislodge a clot and is unsafe.

questions.prompt: A client presents with fever and a rapidly spreading red, hot area on the lower leg. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Perform lymphatic drainage to 'flush it out'.
choices.B: Use friction over the area to increase circulation.
choices.C: Apply heat and continue the session.
choices.D: Do not treat; refer for prompt medical evaluation for possible infection.

questions.answer: Do not treat; refer for prompt medical evaluation for possible infection.
questions.why: Signs of infection require medical evaluation; massage over/around an infected area can worsen spread and is contraindicated.
questions.trap: Avoiding the area still ignores systemic risk and possible contagion.

questions.prompt: A client presents with fever and a rapidly spreading red, hot area on the lower leg. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Apply heat and continue the session.
choices.B: Do not treat; refer for prompt medical evaluation for possible infection.

questions.answer: Do not treat; refer for prompt medical evaluation for possible infection.

questions.prompt: A client reports low back pain with radiating leg pain AND new bowel/bladder changes. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Use stretching and continue if pain decreases.
choices.B: Proceed with deep gluteal work to release the sciatic nerve.
choices.C: Stop and refer for urgent medical evaluation (possible cauda equina syndrome).
choices.D: Recommend home exercises and complete the session.

questions.answer: Stop and refer for urgent medical evaluation (possible cauda equina syndrome).
questions.why: Neurologic red flags such as bowel/bladder changes with radicular symptoms warrant urgent medical assessment.
questions.trap: Deep work risks delaying needed care and may worsen symptoms.

questions.prompt: A client arrives for a session and appears intoxicated (slurred speech, impaired balance). What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Use deeper pressure to help them 'sleep it off'.
choices.B: Do not treat; follow clinic policy and arrange safe transportation if needed.
choices.C: Treat only if they sign an additional consent form.
choices.D: Continue but avoid the neck and head.

questions.answer: Do not treat; follow clinic policy and arrange safe transportation if needed.
questions.why: Impaired judgment and safety risk make treatment inappropriate; follow policy and prioritize safety. This distinguishes plausible but unsafe options and keeps scope boundaries.
questions.trap: Consent is not valid if the client is impaired; additional forms don't fix that.

questions.prompt: A client is taking anticoagulants and requests deep tissue on the calves. What is the BEST approach?

choices.A: Proceed with deep stripping because anticoagulants reduce clot risk.
choices.B: Use percussive techniques to improve circulation.
choices.C: Perform aggressive friction to break up adhesions.
choices.D: Use lighter pressure and avoid aggressive techniques; monitor closely for bruising and pain.

questions.answer: Use lighter pressure and avoid aggressive techniques; monitor closely for bruising and pain.
questions.why: Anticoagulants increase bruising/bleeding risk; modify pressure and avoid aggressive techniques. This prioritizes safety and appropriate referral/modification.
questions.trap: Deep stripping increases bruising/hematoma risk.

choices.A: Perform aggressive friction to break up adhesions.

questions.trap: Deep stripping increases bruising/hematoma risk.

questions.prompt: A client has a large fresh bruise and asks you to work directly over it. What is the MOST appropriate response?

choices.A: Avoid direct work over the bruise and modify the session to adjacent areas.
choices.B: Proceed normally because bruises are harmless.
choices.C: Use deep pressure to 'break up' the bruise.
choices.D: Apply heat and deep massage directly on it.

questions.answer: Avoid direct work over the bruise and modify the session to adjacent areas.
questions.why: Fresh bruising indicates tissue damage; direct pressure can worsen bleeding and pain.
questions.trap: Trying to 'break up' a bruise is unsafe and may increase tissue trauma.

questions.prompt: A client has a large fresh bruise and asks you to work directly over it. What is the MOST appropriate response? (choose the best answer)

choices.A: Apply heat and deep massage directly on it.
choices.B: Use friction to speed healing.
choices.C: Use deep pressure to 'break up' the bruise.
choices.D: Avoid direct work over the bruise and modify the session to adjacent areas.

questions.answer: Avoid direct work over the bruise and modify the session to adjacent areas.
questions.why: Fresh bruising indicates tissue damage; direct pressure can worsen bleeding and pain. This distinguishes plausible but unsafe options and keeps scope boundaries.
questions.trap: Trying to 'break up' a bruise is unsafe and may increase tissue trauma.

questions.prompt: A client reports they took a strong pain medication and cannot accurately feel pressure. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Perform aggressive stretching to improve range of motion.
choices.B: Rely on your judgment and avoid asking for feedback.
choices.C: Use very conservative pressure with frequent check-ins, or reschedule if safe feedback is not possible.
choices.D: Increase intensity to maximize results while numb.

questions.answer: Use very conservative pressure with frequent check-ins, or reschedule if safe feedback is not possible.
questions.why: If sensation is impaired, the client cannot reliably guide pressure; conservative work or rescheduling reduces injury risk.
questions.trap: Numbness increases risk of tissue injury because pain feedback is blunted.

questions.prompt: A client reports unusual bruising since starting a new medication. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Ignore it unless bruises are painful.
choices.B: Use friction over bruised areas to reduce discoloration.
choices.C: Tell them to stop the medication before the next session.
choices.D: Use gentle techniques and recommend they discuss the bruising with their healthcare provider.

questions.answer: Use gentle techniques and recommend they discuss the bruising with their healthcare provider.
questions.why: New unexplained bruising may indicate medication side effects or bleeding risk; gentle care and referral to prescriber is appropriate.
questions.trap: Advising medication changes is outside scope; refer to the prescriber.

questions.prompt: A client has an open wound covered by a dressing on the lower leg. What is the BEST approach?

choices.A: Avoid the wound area and maintain hygiene; modify treatment to protect tissue integrity.
choices.B: Remove the dressing to assess the wound.
choices.C: Work directly over the dressing to reduce swelling.
choices.D: Proceed normally because it is covered.

questions.answer: Avoid the wound area and maintain hygiene; modify treatment to protect tissue integrity.
questions.why: Open wounds increase infection risk and are contraindicated for direct work; maintain boundaries and avoid the area.
questions.trap: Removing dressings is outside scope and risks contamination.

questions.prompt: A client has inflamed varicose veins and requests deep stripping over them. What is the MOST appropriate response?

choices.A: Avoid deep work over varicose veins and use gentle techniques or work elsewhere.
choices.B: Use vigorous friction to break up the veins.
choices.C: Perform percussion over the area to stimulate circulation.
choices.D: Apply heat and deep massage directly on the veins.

questions.answer: Avoid deep work over varicose veins and use gentle techniques or work elsewhere.
questions.why: Varicosities are fragile; aggressive pressure increases risk of pain, bruising, and vascular injury.
questions.trap: Deep stripping can damage fragile vessels and worsen symptoms.

questions.prompt: A client has a new rash that appears contagious and is not covered by clothing. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Reschedule and follow infection-control policy; advise medical evaluation if needed.
choices.B: Apply topical cream and continue.
choices.C: Proceed but avoid the rash area.
choices.D: Wear gloves and continue the session as normal.

questions.answer: Reschedule and follow infection-control policy; advise medical evaluation if needed.
questions.why: Potentially contagious rashes require rescheduling to protect others and follow hygiene policy. This distinguishes plausible but unsafe options and keeps scope boundaries.
questions.trap: Avoiding the area does not eliminate contagion risk.

questions.prompt: A pregnant client reports dizziness when lying supine. What is the BEST modification?

choices.A: Continue supine but work faster to finish the session.
choices.B: Use prone positioning with firm pressure for support.
choices.C: Use side-lying or supported semi-reclined positioning to avoid supine hypotension.
choices.D: Keep the client supine and increase pillows under the head only.

questions.answer: Use side-lying or supported semi-reclined positioning to avoid supine hypotension.
questions.why: Supine positioning can compress major vessels; side-lying or semi-reclined positioning improves comfort and safety.
questions.trap: Pillows under head alone may not relieve vena cava compression.

questions.prompt: An older adult client has very fragile skin and bruises easily. What is the BEST approach?

choices.A: Use vigorous percussion to stimulate circulation.
choices.B: Use gentle techniques, reduce friction, and frequently reassess comfort and skin response.
choices.C: Proceed with strong pressure since bruising is normal with age.
choices.D: Avoid all massage and refer out immediately.

questions.answer: Use gentle techniques, reduce friction, and frequently reassess comfort and skin response.
questions.why: Fragile skin increases risk of tears and bruising; gentle pressure and reduced friction are appropriate.
questions.trap: Strong pressure increases injury risk and is not necessary.

questions.prompt: A client with osteoporosis requests aggressive deep tissue over the spine. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Decline aggressive spinal work and modify with gentle techniques; consider medical clearance if needed.
choices.B: Use high-velocity thrusts to improve mobility.
choices.C: Use heavy percussion directly over the spine.
choices.D: Proceed with deep pressure to strengthen bone.

questions.answer: Decline aggressive spinal work and modify with gentle techniques; consider medical clearance if needed.
questions.why: Osteoporosis increases fracture risk; avoid aggressive pressure over bony prominences and modify safely. This distinguishes plausible but unsafe options and keeps scope boundaries.
questions.trap: Deep pressure does not strengthen bone and may cause injury.

questions.prompt: A client with diabetes reports decreased sensation in the feet. What is the BEST modification?

choices.A: Use stronger pressure since reduced sensation lowers discomfort.
choices.B: Use lighter pressure and frequent check-ins; inspect for skin issues and avoid compromised areas.
choices.C: Use heat packs to increase circulation regardless of sensation.
choices.D: Ignore foot sensation changes as long as pulses are present.

questions.answer: Use lighter pressure and frequent check-ins; inspect for skin issues and avoid compromised areas.
questions.why: Neuropathy reduces protective sensation; gentle pressure and skin checks reduce injury risk. This prioritizes safety and appropriate referral/modification.
questions.trap: Reduced sensation increases risk of burns and tissue damage from heat or pressure.

questions.prompt: During a session, a diabetic client becomes sweaty, shaky, and confused. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Tell them to drive home and eat.
choices.B: Continue the session to help them relax.
choices.C: Apply heat to raise blood sugar.
choices.D: Stop the session and follow hypoglycemia protocol (provide fast sugar if appropriate and seek help if not improving).

questions.trap: Continuing delays treatment and can be dangerous.

questions.prompt: A client in active cancer treatment reports extreme fatigue and lowered immunity. What is the BEST approach?

choices.A: Use intense friction over lymph nodes to 'boost immunity'.
choices.B: Use gentle, comfort-focused care and obtain medical clearance when indicated.
choices.C: Proceed with full-intensity massage to improve strength.
choices.D: Perform vigorous deep tissue to break up adhesions.

questions.answer: Use gentle, comfort-focused care and obtain medical clearance when indicated.
questions.why: Active treatment often requires conservative modifications; gentle comfort care with clearance is appropriate. This distinguishes plausible but unsafe options and keeps scope boundaries.
questions.trap: Aggressive techniques can be overly taxing and unsafe with low immunity.

choices.D: Do not proceed; refer for medical evaluation and reschedule when stable.

questions.answer: Do not proceed; refer for medical evaluation and reschedule when stable.
questions.why: Lightheadedness with uncontrolled hypertension is a safety risk and warrants medical evaluation.
questions.trap: Massage is not a substitute for medical management and could worsen symptoms.

questions.prompt: A client with a seizure disorder reports they missed their medication today. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Use rapid percussion to stimulate the nervous system.
choices.B: Reschedule or proceed only with conservative care and a clear safety plan per policy; stop if any warning signs occur.
choices.C: Encourage them to take double medication now.
choices.D: Proceed with deep pressure to prevent seizures.

questions.answer: Reschedule or proceed only with conservative care and a clear safety plan per policy; stop if any warning signs occur.
questions.why: Missed medication may increase seizure risk; conservative planning or rescheduling is safest within policy. This prioritizes safety and appropriate referral/modification.
questions.trap: Medication advice is outside scope; do not recommend dosing changes.

questions.prompt: A client has pitting edema in both ankles. What is the BEST response?

choices.A: Perform deep stripping over the calves to remove swelling.
choices.B: Diagnose the cause and provide a treatment plan.
choices.C: Ignore it if the client is not in pain.
choices.D: Use conservative care and recommend medical evaluation to identify underlying cause.

questions.answer: Use conservative care and recommend medical evaluation to identify underlying cause.
questions.why: Bilateral pitting edema can indicate systemic issues; recommend medical evaluation and avoid aggressive techniques. This distinguishes plausible but unsafe options and keeps scope boundaries.
questions.trap: Deep work and heat may be inappropriate without understanding the cause.
